raw_text: Spotify should add community tags to songs, similar to Steam tags.Spotify's recommendation system is good, but discovering music by specific characteristics is still difficult.A feature I'd love to see is community-driven tags for songs, similar to how Steam tags games. Users could add tags such as:- Brostep- Female vocals- Melancholic- Heavy bass- Atmospheric- Cybermetal- Jazzetc...The most upvoted tags would appear on the song's page, and users could click any tag to find other songs with the same characteristics, exactly like how steam tags work.This would make music discovery much more precise than relying solely on genres, playlists, and Spotify's algorithm.Steam's tag system works extremely well for helping users find exactly what they're looking for, and I think a similar system could do the same for music.

title: How dp I stop bad recomendations and spotify adfing crap songs to my lists?

raw_text:
Hi there!
 
Could you confirm if you see a + Spotify next to your name when you open a specific playlist? If so, you most likely have Enhance / Smart Shuffle toggled on. Both features add temporary song recommendations to the current playlist and aim to help you discover new music. You can disable them by following the steps below. Doing so removes all temporary suggestions and gets rid of the + Spotify tag.

To disable Enhance, tap the Enhanced button again while in Playlist view.
To disable Smart Shuffle, tap the Shuffle Icon while in Playlist view and either select regular Shuffle or turn it off entirely.

In case you were referring to song recommendations in general, here are a couple of ways you can improve the suggestions you get from Spotify:
 
1.) Exclude playlist from taste profile
You can exclude playlists you aren't interested in so that the app doesn't suggest similar content in the future:

Open the playlist and tap the three dots menu (right-click if you're on desktop).
Select Exclude from taste profile.

2.) Hide song / Don't play this artist
On the mobile app, you can hide songs songs you don't want the app playing by tapping the three dots menu > Hide song . You can also open the artist's profile and select Don't play this artist.
 
3.) Not interested
On the mobile app, you can curate the content in the Music subfeed of your Home screen. In case you find a suggestion that doesn't interest you, you can mark is as Not interested. More info on that here.
 
Hope this info was useful. Cheers!

raw_text: The desktop app used to provide quick and reliable access to recently played albums and playlists through a dedicated Recents section on the Home page. In recent versions, the Recents row appears inconsistently and often disappears altogether, making it much harder to return to content that users have recently been listening to.I would like Spotify to restore a permanent, always-visible Recents row on the desktop Home page, similar to the experience offered in the mobile app. This section should remain available regardless of what recommendations, personalised playlists, podcasts, audiobooks or other promotional content Spotify chooses to display elsewhere on the Home page.Many users use the Home page primarily as a starting point for returning to albums and playlists they already know and listen to regularly. Having to search through activity feeds or recommendation modules to find recently used content adds unnecessary friction to a task that should be immediate.Please restore a dedicated, permanently visible Recents row on the desktop Home page.

raw_text: Dear Spotify Team,I have been a Spotify Premium subscriber for many years and have always appreciated the platform's ability to help me discover new music and artists. Unfortunately, over the past several months, I've noticed a significant decline in that experience.Many of the playlists and recommendations being presented to me now feel repetitive and stale. Different playlists often contain many of the same songs, resulting in very little actual music discovery. Instead of being introduced to new artists or tracks, I find myself hearing the same selections over and over again.Additionally, the genres and artists being recommended increasingly do not align with my listening habits. It feels as though the algorithm has become less accurate and less personalized than it once was. Rather than helping me explore new music within the genres I enjoy, it often suggests music that has little relevance to my tastes.One of the primary reasons I subscribe to a music streaming service is to discover new music. Lately, that aspect of Spotify feels largely absent, and the overall listening experience has become predictable and, frankly, boring.At the same time, subscription prices continue to increase. As a paying customer, I expect ongoing improvements to the service, particularly in areas like personalization and music discovery. Instead, I feel the experience has moved in the opposite direction.I wanted to provide this feedback because I genuinely enjoy Spotify and would prefer to remain a customer. However, if the recommendation and discovery experience continues to decline, I will seriously consider exploring alternative streaming platforms that do a better job of introducing listeners to new music.Thank you for taking the time to read my feedback. I hope it can be shared with the teams responsible for recommendations, playlist curation, and user experience.

raw_text: Current Behaviour:Right now, Spotify's automated discovery algorithms (Discover Weekly, Release Radar, Smart Shuffle, and Autoplay) default heavily toward mainstream global hits, which are overwhelmingly dominated by American artists. Even if a user’s personal listening history consists primarily of UK, European, or other regional music, the algorithm frequently overrides these habits to push US-centric music. There is currently no setting to specify which geographic music scenes you want to discover or prioritise.My Suggestion for Improvement:I propose adding a "Preferred Regions/Locales" setting within the app preferences, or a localized filter toggle directly on automated playlists.This feature would allow users to select specific regions (e.g., "UK & Ireland", "Europe", "Nordic", etc.) that they want the algorithm to actively prioritize. Selecting a region would adjust the recommendation weights, preventing the feed from constantly defaulting to American charts.As a global platform, Spotify should allow listeners to explicitly support and discover talent from their own regions or specific cultures they are interested in, rather than pushing a one-size-fits-all, US-heavy music feed.

There is currently no setting to specify which geographic music scenes you want to discover or prioritise.My Suggestion for Improvement:I propose adding a "Preferred Regions/Locales" setting within the app preferences, or a localized filter toggle directly on automated playlists.This feature would allow users to select specific regions (e.g., "UK & Ireland", "Europe", "Nordic", etc.) that they want the algorithm to actively prioritize. Selecting a region would adjust the recommendation weights, preventing the feed from constantly defaulting to American charts.As a global platform, Spotify should allow listeners to explicitly support and discover talent from their own regions or specific cultures they are interested in, rather than pushing a one-size-fits-all, US-heavy music feed.
title: Regional Focus: Allow users to select preferred locales/regions for algorithm recommendations

raw_text: Currently, temporary listening habits—like playing white noise for sleep, kids' songs, or exploring a new genre briefly—permanently mess up the recommendation algorithm (Discover Weekly, Release Radar, etc.). Right now, there is no way to refresh our recommendations. We either have to endure irrelevant music for months or create a brand new account, which forces us to lose all our carefully curated playlists and liked songs.I suggest adding a simple "Reset Recommendations" or "Clear Listening Data" button under Settings > Privacy or Account. Clicking this should wipe the historical listening data used for the algorithm, giving users a clean slate for their personalized mixes, while safely keeping all saved playlists, followed artists, and liked songs intact.

This becomes an issue in cases where:An account is shared between multiple users with very different music tastesA user’s preferences significantly change over timeThe account has been used temporarily by someone elseIn such situations, recommendations become inaccurate and no longer reflect the primary user’s preferences.Proposed Solution:Introduce a “Reset Listening History” or “Start Fresh” option in account settings that:Clears all listening historyResets algorithmic recommendations (Discover Weekly, Daily Mixes, etc.)Optionally allows users to retain saved songs and playlistsBenefits:Gives users control over their recommendation systemImproves personalization accuracyHelps users who share accounts or undergo taste changesEnhances overall user satisfactionAdditional Suggestion (Optional):Spotify could also consider introducing separate user profiles under one Premium account, similar to other streaming platforms, to avoid this issue altogether.Thanks for considering this feature!

raw_text: Why not, I'll pile on too... I use Spotify *only* for Discover Weekly; the secret is out that anyone who just wants to listen to a song can find it on YouTube.  But Discover Weekly was great for years for me!  I looked forward to sitting down with my guitar and Discover Weekly every week and learning new music. So about six months ago, when my Discover Weekly got irrecoverably stuck on electronica because I listened *once* to *one song* that was *vaguely* electronic (my nine-year-old was involved in this decision), I tried everything I could to get out of this hole.  I liked a zillion songs, I played a zillion songs, I created playlists, I clicked the "remove" button on dozens of the electronic songs that came up... no dice, still just a new electronica playlist every week. (With, strangely, a cover of "You're The Best" from The Karate Kid mixed in once.  That I can't explain.) So, I'm giving up and deleting this account, then re-importing those playlists into a new account, and hoping Discover Weekly gets the message.  Recommender systems are hard, we get it.  The fact that this happens isn't a catastrophe, somehow that one song got a zillion recommender-weight-points or whatever.  But why would I not be able to just delete my play history and start over?  Wouldn't that be easier for Spotify to implement than trying to fix the black box that is the recommendation engine so this never happens?  Urrrrrgh.

raw_text: Customer requests to fix Spotify's built-in genre trolling problem go back for years. I've been holding on for several years now hoping that this would improve because I was a huge fan when they were a startup, but it's a different company now, and they've pretty plainly said that they're never going to change this. It's part of their income, so makes sense from a shorter-term financial perspective even if it doesn't make any sense from a longer-term customer service and churn perspective. I'd hang on to a free account for commercial applications like office lobby/grocery store background music or something, but for a normal, personalized and fun music listening experience, it's worth taking the time to explore the better options out there. Just my  2 cents.

raw_text: Even though I've never listened to a podcast on my Spotify account, Spotify keeps recommending me multiple podcasts that I've absolutely no interest in. And what's more bothering is that you can't remove them from your home page or block podcast suggestions completely at the moment. I've also chatted with Spotify support and they told me it's not possible to remove podcast suggestions from home screen completely, and they suggested I wrote down my complaint on the community page so that users with similar complaints can make sure their voices are heard. So please, if you're having the same problem as me, leave a remark on this and try to enhance user experience who only enjoy music and are not interested in listening to lame people yap about trivial stuff and think this is a good idea. Edit: Hey everyone, it's relieving to see so many people sharing similar ideas about the horrific recommendation system Spotify has been implementing. Despite the fact that I have never, ever listened to a single minute of podcast, I still get recommended stoic mindset bulls*** or stand-up comedy or movie review podcasts. If I wanted to listen to a movie critique or wanted to watch or listen to a stand-up comedy show without interruption,  I'd pay for YouTube premium, not you damn Spotify. I wanna stream music and only MUSIC!!! Fix it!

raw_text: Hi Spotify team, I'm a long-time Spotify user, and I really enjoy the AI DJ feature. It’s fun, seamless, and adds a personal touch to my listening experience. One improvement I’d love to see is customisation options for the DJ’s voice. Currently, the AI DJ has a consistent (and great) voice, but it would be amazing to have the ability to:Choose between different accents (e.g. Australian, RP, Irish, etc.)Select a female voice option, or even other voice types/personalitiesThis would make the feature feel even more personal and engaging, and give listeners the ability to tailor it to their preferences, just like we do with music.I think a lot of users would appreciate this level of personalisation, so I hope it’s something you’ll consider adding in a future update. Thanks for all your great work

Hi.How do I hide the "Recommended Songs" tab below playlists I created myself?Until now, I always kept this feature hidden, because it bothers me when I scroll to the end of my playlist and there are songs listed that I did not specifically add myself. However, since the latest update, the recommended songs section is being displayed again and I no longer see any button or anything similar to hide it. I also looked through the general Spotify settings but couldn't seem to find any option to turn off that feature or at least hide that section below my playlists there either.Despite not being able to hide it, I can refresh the recommended songs and interact with them as usual. I don't have autoplay or the enhance feature turned on, so I don't see any reason why it might be necessary for the recommended songs section to be displayed.

title: How to hide "Recommended Songs" for personal playlists?

raw_text:
No problem!It is not possible to minimize the recommendations below but if you often scroll down to the bottom, you can instead sort the playlist in the reverse of the current order and it will bring you the bottom stuff on the top. In that way, you need not scroll all the way down and see the recommendations again. I understand this is not the ideal solution you were hoping for, but let me know if you need anything else. Happy to assist!

raw_text: Hi there, I have been wanting to give feedback for months. Something very bad is happening with your recommendation systems. Discovery and serendipity have dropped dramatically and it is horrible. It means I am in an echo chamber of music. Not only that, but another weird thing I have noticed is that when I put on my 60's/70's rock playlist, when it ends it moves onto recommended tracks. This used to continue on with the same taste profile of the playlist itself which often would surface music along the same profile and was a great outcome. In the last 3-6 months I have noticed when a playlist ends, it recommended tracks reverts to my recent listening history and regurgitates what I already listen to. So in the case of this 60's/70's rock playlist, when it ends, it plays maybe 1-2 songs of 60's/70's rock and then dives into my overall music taste of deep house and it just plays deep house tracks from my 2026 Summer playlist. This is such a jarring and glaring issue. Your recommendation engines are not doing well and I urge you to revert to an older model. The new one IS NOT GOOD.

It’s easier for other users to know what you’re suggesting so they can add their support and feedback. It’s also easier for us to share updates and talk to the right teams at Spotify. raw_text:
Hey @pavowren,
 
Thanks for reaching out!
  
Spotify doesn’t play or include ads that interrupt the music listening experience of Premium subscribers. However, you might still see sponsored content in other areas of the app. This might include personalized recommendations based on your listening history, or you might get a banner letting you know you're also eligible for other Premium subscriptions, such as e.g. Premium Duo or Family. Nevertheless, these are just recommendations and will not interrupt your ad-free music listening time 
 As for the 'Not Interested' button seemingly not doing anything, we do thank you for bringing this to our attention. 
 In case there's anything else in the meantime, don't hesitate to reach back to us. We're just a post away

Thank you for reporting this and for the additional info!On my end the episodes disappeared when I de-selected and re-selected the setting from the mobile app. Could you try doing the same?If this doesn't help out, we can try some troubleshooting instead - see if clearing the cached data on your device would make a difference. Log out of the app and back in twice in a row after this to trigger a manual sync of your settings and data. In case the issue remains - see if going for a complete clean reinstall of the app would change things for you.

raw_text: After six months of Discover Weekly trying to convert me, I have come to the conclusion that my Spotify account may be possessed by the spirit of Jesus Christ.Every Monday I perform an exorcism by hiding tracks, skipping songs and blocking artists. Yet the following week He returns. Stronger. More determined. More CEDM.Meanwhile, Daylist, AI DJ and my Mixes understand my taste perfectly. Only Discover Weekly seems convinced that my true calling is "Jesus Is The King (D&B Remix)".My suggestion: please give users the option to block an entire genre from recommendations. Sometimes I don't dislike a few artists—I simply never want recommendations from a specific genre cluster.For now I have blocked every Christian EDM artist I could find. If next week's Discover Weekly is still full of worship drum & bass, I will assume Spotify's recommendation engine has achieved sentience and is actively trying to save my soul.

title: Can't play an artist's discography on the desktop app

raw_text:
Hi again, @Nostalgia85,
 
I think what's happening is that you might be loading only one song in the queue and that's why the playback stops when you press "skip next" - there aren't any other songs added in the queue to be played.
 
Here's an excerpt from the aforementioned FAQ article on how to play an artist's discography on desktop:
 
While on the discography page of an artist, select your preferred view from the button on the right:
 

 
Then, depending on the view you've selected:

List view: Click on either the white Play button of the album at the top or double-click on the first song - this will add the rest of the artist's discography to the queue.
Grid view: Hover over the album that's at the top and press the big green Play button - the remaining albums will be queued as well.

 Tip: you can also reverse the order in which the albums are displayed by selecting the same sort order again.
 
That should do the trick. If you're still having trouble, feel free to share a short screen recording which captures the exact behavior on your end so we can take a closer look.

raw_text: Spotify Desktop version: 1.2.90.451.gb094aab0Operating system: Windows 11Spotify plan: Premium Hey, I encountered a problem when trying to play artist’s whole discography on Spotify desktop app. Previously I’ve searched the artist I want to listen to and then navigated to their discography view where their albums are listed in chronological order from latest to oldest by release date. From there I’ve just selected the song from a particular album where I want to start listening and after that album ends the Spotify automatically moves to the next album in the discography. And this had worked perfectly up to this point. Vut now Spotify doesn’t automatically move to the next album in the discography. Instead of moving to the next album in the discography once the album I have started listening to ends, Spotify starts playing songs from similar artists. This is the case when Autoplay is turned on. If I toggle Autoplay off, then Spotify just starts the album from the beginning when it ends and doesn’t move to the next album in the discography. This only happens on the Desktop app, the web player works just fine and moves to the next album in the discography when the previous one ends. Any ideas on why this happens and how to fix it? And please do not suggest creating separate playlists for each artist and then adding their songs into them or manually adding songs into queue, these aren’t proper solutions for this issue.

raw_text: Hello Spotify Team,I would like to share feedback regarding the current artist and album listening experience in the desktop application.For years, many users requested better discography browsing, especially the ability to view and explore releases in chronological order. Spotify's community forums contain years of feedback asking for proper oldest-to-newest sorting, and more recently improvements were introduced to make discography navigation much better.As someone who enjoys discovering artists through their complete body of work, this was exactly the kind of improvement I had been waiting for.Unfortunately, the value of these changes now feels reduced because the playback experience increasingly pushes listeners away from album-focused listening.I often want to start with an artist's earliest album and continue through their catalog, album by album, experiencing their evolution over time. The recent addition of chronological sorting finally made that possible from a navigation perspective.However, when playback behavior starts steering users toward autoplay, artist radio, recommendations, or algorithm-generated content, it works against the purpose of exploring a discography in the first place.If I open an artist page, sort releases from oldest to newest, and start listening to albums, my intention is very clear: I want to listen to that artist's albums.Not radio.Not recommendations.Not a personalized stream.If I want artist radio, I will deliberately start artist radio.What makes this especially frustrating is that Spotify has already invested effort into improving discography organization and chronological browsing. Those were positive changes that acknowledged how many listeners still care about albums as complete works. The playback experience should reinforce that goal rather than interrupt it.I would love to see Spotify continue improving album-focused listening by:Supporting seamless album-to-album discography playback.Respecting chronological listening workflows.Keeping album listening clearly separated from recommendation-driven listening.Bringing advanced discography features to mobile apps rather than simplifying desktop behavior toward a more radio-focused experience.Albums are one of the foundations of music culture. Many listeners still enjoy experiencing music the way artists released it, one record at a time.Thank you for your consideration.PlanPremiumCountryPL Operating System(iWindows 11) My Question or Issue

The playback experience should reinforce that goal rather than interrupt it.I would love to see Spotify continue improving album-focused listening by:Supporting seamless album-to-album discography playback.Respecting chronological listening workflows.Keeping album listening clearly separated from recommendation-driven listening.Bringing advanced discography features to mobile apps rather than simplifying desktop behavior toward a more radio-focused experience.Albums are one of the foundations of music culture. Many listeners still enjoy experiencing music the way artists released it, one record at a time.Thank you for your consideration.PlanPremiumCountryPL Operating System(iWindows 11) My Question or Issue

title: How can I remove the “playing recommended songs for you” feature

raw_text:
I finally solved this problem. It was incredibly irritating and I read all the You tube posts etc which didn't apply to me. I figured it out yesterday. Relief!!!!!Your random play icon that looks like two curved arrows that cross actually has 3 functions, not 2. When turned off, it will play your playlist in order. When turned ON, there are actually 2 ONS. One is to randomize your playlist and one is to randomize your playlist AS WELL AS adds recommendations. The two functions look almost exactly alike (they light up green when on) but one has a little dash on top of it and when you click on this option it actually does read that it will add recommendation. good luck all. I was so relieved to figure this out and enjoy my own playlist again!

title: How can I remove the “playing recommended songs for you” feature

raw_text:
Hi there!
 
Which devices does this behavior occur on? Does it occur only on the desktop or also on the mobile app? Have you tried using the Web Player instead to see if there's a difference? A screenshot of what you're referring to would be greatly appreciated as we can get a clear idea of how things look on your end.
 
In general, if you're using the Free version of Spotify on your phone, and your playlist has less than 15 tracks in it, the app will automatically add new songs to the playlist so that its runtime increases and you don't get the same songs twice. This feature cannot be disabled, but if you add more than 15 songs, the additions from Spotify would disappear.
 

 
Premium users have access to the Enhance playlist feature, which automatically adds similar songs to your playlist as long as the feature is turned on. You can turn it off by clicking on the Enhance button at the top of your playlist.
 

 
Something else that's worth checking out is if the Autoplay feature is toggled on in the app's Settings and toggle it off. Autoplay continues the playback with similar songs once your current music finishes playing. More info can be found here.
 

 
Let us know how it goes. We'll be on the lookout for your reply.
 
Cheers!

title: How can I remove the “playing recommended songs for you” feature

raw_text:
Hey @pcmcdonnell,
 
Welcome to the Community and thank you for joining the conversation. 
 
If you don't have the Enhance button, it's possible that the recommendations were added by the Smart Shuffle feature. This feature adds some recommended tracks to the playlist where it was activated and it's only available for Premium users.
 
To turn it off, you just need to tap the Shuffle button again to gray it out. In case you want to use the regular shuffle function, make sure to select the correct option when tapping the Shuffle button again. You'll find more info in this article. 
Hope this helps. Let us know how it goes.

title: How can I remove the “playing recommended songs for you” feature

raw_text:
Hey folks,
 
"Playing recommended songs for you" is caused by the Autoplay feature. If Autoplay on desktop is controlling the feature's behavior across all platforms, try disabling it on the desktop app to see if that does the trick.
 
Smart Shuffle is another feature that keeps your listening sessions fresh by mixing in recommendations that match the vibe and if you've enabled it, Spotify will add tracks and personalized recommendations.
 
 
If you're still seeing songs in the queue that you don't recognize or haven't personally added, I'd recommend checking out this article, specifically the 'Why do I see songs in the queue I haven't personally added?' for more info on what to do next.
 
Hope this clears things up. Cheers!

raw_text: I was pulling my hair out when this app stopped playing my playlist and instead created its own recommended queue. I’d restart the app and it would return to the same dang place. Finally figured out under account settings there’s a Playback > Autoplay Similar Content toggle, which I turned off. Reading the unreliable shuffle/enhanced shuffle selection, I finally turned shuffle off and just sorted the playlist by song title instead, which breaks up clusters of album selections. Good luck with the bug-riddled playback! Hope this helps.

raw_text: There is really a very simple answer to this, which is for Spotify to make their “recommendations an opt-in rather than a default. I assume this would mean that if Spotify’s recommendation algorithm continues to be terrible, users would know that they opted in and that there would be a simple box to uncheck to remove this “feature” for them. The suggestions given on this string gave me a way to stop the recommendations, for which I am grateful. However, not everyone is comfortable or confident in getting into the many layers of settings, across platforms. Put most simply, surprising users with a terrible algorithm creating content for them is a very bad way to do business. Make it voluntary, not default.

raw_text: I finally solved this problem. It was incredibly irritating and I read all the You tube posts etc which didn't apply to me. I figured it out yesterday. Relief!!!!!Your random play icon that looks like two curved arrows that cross actually has 3 functions, not 2. When turned off, it will play your playlist in order. When turned ON, there are actually 2 ONS. One is to randomize your playlist and one is to randomize your playlist AS WELL AS adds recommendations. The two functions look almost exactly alike (they light up green when on) but one has a little dash on top of it and when you click on this option it actually does read that it will add recommendation. good luck all. I was so relieved to figure this out and enjoy my own playlist again!

title: Shuffle mode playing the same songs all the time

raw_text: Hi @Tom7777777, Thanks for posting in the Spotify Community! Unfortunately, in large playlists (over 150 songs), the shuffle option prioritizes the songs that Spotify perceives you enjoy the most; this is why you might notice that some songs are repeated while listening on shuffle. However, there is currently a Live Idea to have an option for a “true” shuffle, which would play all the songs in a playlist in a random order, and once they’re all played, reshuffle them and play them again. If this is something you’re interested in, I’d recommend adding your +VOTE to this idea; the more votes an idea gets, the likelier it is that Spotify will implement it, and you can read more about how feedback on ideas reaches Spotify here. It will also be useful for you to Subscribe to the idea by clicking the three dots next to its title; that way, you can be sure to be informed of any relevant updates. Let me know if you have any other questions, and have a good day!

If this is something you’re interested in, I’d recommend adding your +VOTE to this idea; the more votes an idea gets, the likelier it is that Spotify will implement it, and you can read more about how feedback on ideas reaches Spotify here. It will also be useful for you to Subscribe to the idea by clicking the three dots next to its title; that way, you can be sure to be informed of any relevant updates. Let me know if you have any other questions, and have a good day!

raw_text:
Hi there @Supercool131,
 
Thank you for your reply.
 
Some devices can't handle playlists that are this big , so in order to improve compatibility with devices of all types, the app caches and pre-loads "chunks" of playlists. It then tries to prioritize the most favored songs as previously described.
 
With time and optimizations, this should become better. In the meantime we'd recommend to split playlists up into more manageable parts. 
 
We hope this clear things up 

Let us know if we can help you with anything else.
 
Take care!

raw_text: Alright everyone, I ran some experiments and here’s what I found:- you can have a maximum of 430ish songs before Spotify repeats the queue regardless if you have more songs- if you skip a song it will still counts towards the 430~ since one of the other stupid changes Spotify made, other than ruining shuffle, is that even if a song plays for one second, it counts as recently played (I wonder how this will mess up Spotify wrap this year).- if you skip a song within the queue and bypass it, it won’t count, and I was actually able to get to the end of a playlist that way.- I’ve seen people say that when they use Spotify AI playlists or Spotify DJ they don’t have that issue anymore, and this has made me realize that the reason Spotify shuffle is broken is probably because they want to force users to use their “smart shuffle” and AI modes and wired shuffle to only work with those, which impacts the rest of us who just want to listen to the playlist WE made Solution: the only way to listen to your playlists normally again, is to switch to another  app. I’m planning on going to , but there’s others like Dzer. Spotify doesn’t care enough about their users to make sure we can enjoy our premium subscription to the fullest, and it’s a waste to keep paying for it if we can’t even listen to our playlists like we used to.

The app literally injects a block of already played history right after the current song, just because I navigated away.

raw_text:
The same thing happened with me I fix it instantly to follow mention below steps.

Check Shuffle Settings: Some music players have different shuffle settings. Ensure yours is set to fully randomize the playlist.
Clear Cache: Sometimes the music player's cache affects playback. Clearing the cache can reset any stuck shuffle patterns.
Update the App: Make sure your music player is updated to the latest version as updates often fix bugs related to shuffle algorithms.
Create Sub-Playlists: Divide your large playlist into smaller ones. This can help the shuffle algorithm work more efficiently and potentially more randomly.
Use Third-Party Apps: If the issue persists, consider using a different music player. Some apps may offer better shuffle algorithms.

These steps should help improve the variety of songs you hear in shuffle mode.

raw_text: Hi,  I've been a Spotify client since about 2016. And I've seen it evolve over time into what it is today. It's a great music app! It's simple to use, the layout is excellent. I don't have any other problems with it besides this reoccurring issue I've been having since I downloaded it in 2016.  I have over 2000+ songs on my "liked songs" playlist, however I only ever hear a handful of songs when I put it on shuffle! I don't hear the rest of my extensive music collection, and it's been getting more and more frustrating to deal with. I want to be able to listen to my whole library without having to break it down into different playlists. I even tried putting all of my liked songs into a different playlist that I created and it still didn't solve the issue. I've noticed in the past that when I look up on google how to solve this problem, there isn't any real solution, yet a lot of the user base have the exact same issue. If so many of us are having the same problem, then perhaps it's time for you to reevaluate how your shuffling system works.  I hope you will take this idea into consideration!

And the wonderful Spotify devs and managers have been so generous in making even the shuffle on Folder of Sub-Folders and playlists for a total of over 200K tracks of varied songs and genres and albums and artists work perfectly!-- until it breaks-- and then we have to go to our user fix sheets and user programming fixes to recover a perfect shuffle again-- So Thank you, thank you all! Summary:  We Roadie accounts that get perfect shuffles until the shuffle breaks have had to create for ourselves different varieties of means for fixing the shuffle-- by-- Skip the next track from the top of the queue if that track has played in the last 90 days-- Or some number of days that the user sets when the user initializes a true shuffle with no advertising gimmicks or payola gimmicks or (whatever it does that Spotify does instead of just the randomness of any string of digits in any garden variety irrational number). That above paragraph summarizes the logic of the simple fix for all of these complaints about the Spotify shuffle.  And that above summary paragraph summarizes the workarounds that all of us Roadies use for getting successful shuffles for 10,000 tracks at a time. Spotify already has a check on whether the Next track in the queue is to be skipped because the license to play has expired-- So similarly Spotify could make a very needed programming fix for all of these frustrating and infuriating repeats and repeats of repeats-- just by similarly in tandem skipping the play of that next track if it has been played already by this current account in the last 90 days-- or the number of days set by the user when the user selects TrueRandom shuffling. Thanks for all of your ideas here-- which all of my family accounts have taken to heart-- and hence by using all of your ideas here-- All of my family accounts get perfect shuffles when they 1) notice immediately when the shuffle has broken-- and then 2) initialize the next true shuffle-- by blasting delete all of those intermediate files that store the repeats that infuriate all of us when the shuffle breaks-- and then 3) copy to an alert file the first 81 tracks of the visible queue-- because most of the time when the shuffle breaks, the Spotify shuffle comes out of the state of confusion-- like after losing the Wi-Fi connection-- and in confusion starts playing the last successful shuffle from the start-- and that is really infuriating-- so you retain that copy of the top of the last successful shuffle as part of your diagnostic check to compare with the queue to notice immediately when the shuffle has broken as many people have said in this and similar forums over and over to warn us all-- Thank you!

raw_text: I listen to Spotify a lot on my Android and my TV. I've discovered that shuffle is not truly shuffle. When I select a playlist, it plays the songs in a set order, not in a shuffle order. So if I listen to a playlist that has about 600 songs when I'm driving, I'll hear a certain number of songs. When I get back in my car and put that same playlist on, it will start again and go through the same songs. It's not truly shuffling. Why does it insist on doing this and why can't it be fixed? Same thing happens if I choose to listen a certain artist. It only plays a select number of songs, not all the songs that the artist has released. Is it really that difficult to fix this issue for an app that has been around for so long.

raw_text: The issue with Spotify's random shuffle seems to be related to it's recommendations and listening history. As long as it uses this, it could never be truly random. Using the Like system and liking any songs will absolutely ruin random shuffle for instance. The fewer repeat feature accomplishes nothing as it still cycles through the same songs on reset. You can try training the algorithm by clicking individual songs, but it is very slow at adapting. That is not all because it prioritizes songs based on their own recommendation algorithm. Some newly added songs to the playlist will mysteriously play right away when some others never seem to show up. The issue isn't fewer repeats and whatever "more human" is supposed to mean here. You need to simply not weigh in listening history and your own recommendation algorithm in random shuffle and offer "true random". I created a new small playlist and hitting play resulted in playing the exact same song as the very first. That is obviously not random when the first song is the exact same one. How about a simple playtest where the first song being played is actually random? Fewer repeats only after the first set of the same 100 songs is not what we want. Allow the option to not use listening history and your own damn recommendations. It has nothing to do with device limits and smaller chunks of data. It has to do with you forcing and weighing certain songs over others. Just like your limited selection of recommended tracks offering the same group of artists. Let us opt-out of your tracking years of our listening history. I think you know exactly what the problem is, but refuse to offer a true solution.

raw_text: I've been wanting to talk about this for longest, since this has kind of been a problem for me within the past few years of me using Spotify. This may not exactly be an idea, but I just really want to talk about it.  For context, I mostly use Spotify on my PS5 when I'm gaming and I think it's really great to use as it is free to use on there and doesn't require a subscription like Apple Music does, and I hope it stays that way. My main issue is with the shuffle option and how it does not actually shuffle the songs that are in queue. Mind you I have over 1,092 songs saved on my 'Liked Song' list, and it feels like I'm constantly getting the exactly same songs or songs that are similar to them, which is strange because I thought that 'shuffle' was completely random, but apparently shuffle the feature might run off of some kind of statistic, like songs that you play the most or something like that, which heard on either here or, and it probably explains why I wouldn't hear a song from an artist for months on my list and IMO completely defeats the purpose of having shuffle if it doesn't actually shuffle. There would be many times when I would even get the same exact songs in one session, and I'm like “did I not just hear that song?”. Like yesterday and the day before, I got the exact same queue of shuffled songs, it's that bad.  Please fix the other bugs that may be plaguing Spotify, such as after an advertisement finishing. It just completely freezes, and it doesn't play the next song or next advertisement, and I would have to completely exit Spotify and replay my playlist, and it may not just be Spotify itself or just the version of Spotify that's on PS5. Maybe it could be different; I'm not sure, but I really hope this gets fixed.  Thank you for reading.

raw_text: I have a premium family account and listen to all my 'Liked' songs on shuffle across multiple devices - work laptop, home pc, home stereo (via connect) and phone - and regularly switch which device is playing. I also regularly use each of the different devices to control each other, depending on which device I am listening from vs. which I am using at the time - e.g., in office with work laptop playing, but using my personal PC at the time. Or sitting in the lounge listening on my stereo, but controlling via phone.Recently (last two weeks), I have noticed my shuffle function no longer seems to work properly at all. I get the same pseudo-random songs, in the same order, over and over again. I have noticed this is related to switching devices - at first I thought it was just that each device remembered where it was, and so as I switched play between the devices I was just getting duplicate runs - but after two weeks of the same songs over and over, in the same pseudo-random order, I am more inclined to think this is a bug. (Yes, I like 'Charlie Peacock - Dear Friend, but not multiple times per day, when I have another 7,000 songs I also want to listen to!). It seems like all devices are remembering where the previous device started, and because I switch often (I do the school run and WFH), they are all stuck with the same location/seed?How can I get better random performance? At a guess, it looks like the random function is account-bound (I'm guessing it happens server-side). I want a true random - not this pseudo-random garbage.

raw_text: End the shuffle paradox. I find the "shuffle" feature on Spotify confusing and unhelpful. While it's supposed to determine my most liked songs, it often repeats the same songs repeatedly, defeating the purpose of having a shuffle option. I like other songs, but I can only listen to them if I actively search for them. This means I'm trapped in a cycle of repeatedly listening to the same favourite song, which is pointless. It's a paradox. You select my favourite song via the algorithm you create, but the song you select will forever be my favourite if you keep repeating it and not giving other options! It would be better if Spotify had a true shuffle feature that would allow me to listen to any song in my library and receive a new list every time I select a song. I spend more time skipping songs to hear new ones, which is frustrating. I've even unliked 100 songs to try to change up the shuffle. This became apparent during the end-of-year wrap-up when my most listened to song was the one I skipped the most.

raw_text:
This issue is a duplicate of https://community.spotify.com/t5/Your-Library/shuffle-playlist-seems-to-play-the-same-songs/m-p/5507.... I'm making a new thread, as it was marked as solved, however I don't believe it is, and I have more details to add.
Plan
Premium
Country
us-en
Device
Device a:
Spotify web app (tested this behavior on both Chrome & Firefox).
Device b:
Tested this behavior on both a Google Pixel 7 pro & Google Pixel 8 pro. Last tested on version 9.1.2.1253.
Operating System
Tested this behavior (when using the web app) on both Windows and Ubuntu.
Playlist in Question
Liked Songs
Issue
If songs in a playlist (specifically liked songs, I have not tested extensively with other playlists) are played for a while (with shuffle enabled, not smart shuffle) on one platform (i.e. web player), and then switched to another platform (i.e. mobile app), the same songs are played in the same order starting from where the 2nd platform was originally at.
I have taken statistics classes, I understand "random" does not always mean "evenly shuffled". This however is the same 8 hours of songs in the exact same order. I've been observing this behavior for months now, and it's consistent. It is extremely statistically unlikely this is random behavior. I can look at the "next in queue" page and see the same songs I listened to previously.
I will point out that if I have Spotify open on both Chrome & Firefox, and switch from one to the other, it exhibits this behavior, but upon refreshing the page, the queue syncs and starts playing random songs again.
As a form of confirmation, I've attached a screenshot of my history of songs I listened to yesterday (on Spotify Web Player for Firefox), and my current queue. Shuffle has been enabled the entire time. You will note that (pardon the title) "**bleep**" is not in the queue, because I removed it from my Liked Songs while listening to it yesterday.
I have also tried playing a different playlist, then going back to the liked songs and shuffling them again, and the same queue gets loaded.
P.S. This link: https://community.spotify.com/t5/forums/postpage/board-id/yourlibrary is broken while signed in on Chrome, though it works on Firefox.

raw_text: I'm not talking about it "seeming" like shuffle plays the same songs over and over or even getting 1 or 2 repeats a day which could inherently actually just be due to RNG (but still not likely when my playlist is nearly 3,000 songs), but instead I'm getting literal lists of 20-30 songs in a row that are in *exactly* in the same order.  So admittedly, I have a tendency to skip a lot of songs in a row to find something I'd like to listen to in the moment. I have noticed on more than one occasion (probably half a dozen times now, honestly) over the last month, that the lists I am skipping through are perfect matches to a list I skipped through the previous day or sometimes even as short as an hour prior. When I notice a list of songs that is the same as one I went through recently, I try to break the cycle by manually clicking a new song (rather than using the "next" button) and still find myself back in the same loop within a few songs later.  This is proving that Spotify shuffle is clearly *not* actually shuffling anything, but generating lists ahead of time, repeating them, and hoping you don't notice.  Additionally, I will never use "Smart" shuffle because I do *not* want to listen to songs that are not already on my playlist, so I rely on regular shuffle to actually be shuffled!  This is absolutely a new phenomenon and is seriously annoying, almost to the point of starting to dislike the app.

raw_text: I find that starting to play a different playlist than the one you were playing, and then once again trying to play the playlist you were originally trying to listen to will remake the shuffle 'list'. I have found that if you were attempting to continue to listen to a specific playlist on shuffle, and then for whatever reason you stop it by either exiting or changing devices, and then open the app back up and start trying to listen where you left off, that the shuffle 'list' that you were listening to is still active, and you will either continue listening to that same lined up list, or even backtrack on that same list a bit, which you already heard in that order when you were last listening.We should not have to go about it like this though, especially as paying subscribers. Shuffle should be a true shuffle every time, and honestly there should never be shuffle 'lists'. It should be truly random every time, in accordance with your repeat options. The automix option in the settings should just be a button to toggle right next to the shuffle button on the main playing menu. For me, this was enabled by default, and I could see this contributing to the frustration people are having with shuffle. I keep the auto-mix option off as I already make make organized playlists with similar sounds and feels. A lot of people are probably not even be aware of this option, at all. We want a true shuffle. So give it to us. Sometimes I'll turn on smart shuffle to get some new stuff in the mix, but besides that, I want it completely random. It's not a lot to ask for.

title: Crossfade happening while turned off

raw_text:
Hey @AlKaZ,
 
Thanks for reaching out! 
It's quite odd that the setting won't turn off, this may indicate that something is overwriting your manual changes to the settings. As there seem to be no similar reports on the forum, I'd recommend some troubleshooting here.We can start by logging out everywhere first - you can do so by following this page. Once you're logged out, log in to a single device and check your Crossfade settings. Toggle them on and back off in case they appear off but the app behaviour remains the same.
 
Something else that's worth checking is if you have the Mix feature toggled on: it allows users to create seamless and customizable transitions between the songs in the playlist and you might have accidentally activated it while viewing the playlist. To turn it off, tap the "Mix" button at the top of the playlist again or if you don't see it, look for a "Turn off mix" option in the three dots menu instead.
 
 Tip: When the feature is enabled, the Play button changes its appearance as shown on the screenshot below.
 

 
Should the issue remain, it may be getting caused by something that behaves like the Crossfade setting e.g. a third party app. I'd advise removing them one by one to see if that would make a difference. Any apps can later be re-added at your convenience.
 
Once things are sorted on one of the devices, see if this will be the case for the rest of them as well - log in on them one by one and verify the app's settings and behaviour.
 
Hope this helps! Keep us posted on how things go 

VasilModeratorHelp others find this answer and click "Accept as Solution".If you appreciate an answer, maybe give it a Like.Are you new to the Community? Take a moment to introduce yourself!

To turn it off, tap the "Mix" button at the top of the playlist again or if you don't see it, look for a "Turn off mix" option in the three dots menu instead.
 
 Tip: When the feature is enabled, the Play button changes its appearance as shown on the screenshot below.
 

 
Should the issue remain, it may be getting caused by something that behaves like the Crossfade setting e.g. a third party app. I'd advise removing them one by one to see if that would make a difference. Any apps can later be re-added at your convenience.
 
Once things are sorted on one of the devices, see if this will be the case for the rest of them as well - log in on them one by one and verify the app's settings and behaviour.
 
Hope this helps! Keep us posted on how things go

raw_text: Current experience:When listening to a playlist on Spotify, if I switch to another playlist or album, I lose my exact position in the previous one.When I return, I have to:manually find the trackremember where I stoppedrestart the listening flowThis interrupts the experience, especially when switching between different moods (e.g., work, gym, relaxation). Proposed idea:Add a feature that automatically saves the current state of a playlist when the user switches to another one.This would include:current trackplayback timestampqueue stateshuffle/repeat settingsWhen the user returns to that playlist, it resumes exactly where it was left off. Example:User is listening to Playlist A (Track 10 at 01:32)User switches to Playlist BLater, when returning to Playlist A → it resumes at Track 10, 01:32Where this would apply:Mobile appDesktop appWeb playerWhy this would be useful:Improves continuity of listeningReduces friction when switching between playlistsSupports users with different daily contexts (study, workout, relaxation)Makes playlist listening feel more persistent and personalizedAdditional context:This would work similarly to how media apps remember playback position, but applied to playlists as a continuous listening session.

raw_text: Bumping this because it's still a massive pain point in 2026! The author described the technical side perfectly, and I want to add why this is so frustrating in daily use.Right now, if I'm listening to a massive playlist and just want to check out a single track someone sent me (or one I found via Shazam), I have to manually mess with the "Next Up" queue just to avoid losing my place in the playlist. It’s a clumsy workaround. If I accidentally just click the track, my entire playlist progress is completely wiped out, and I have to manually scroll and guess where I left off.For many of us, having a playlist save its playback state (track, timestamp, and shuffle state) would bring back that rock-solid, reliable experience we used to love in classic media players like Winamp or AIMP.This feature is a must for seamless context-switching between work, gym, and relaxation. Upvoted! I really hope the Spotify team revisits this idea.

raw_text:
Hey @BrandoAZ, welcome to the Spotify Community! 
 
Currently, there is no direct way to do this in the app. So the best way is for you to manually search for the songs you want to add and then save to your library. You can use the desktop app for better usability and more feautres like bulk selection.
 
However, I think you can leverage the new AI Playlist Creation feature. If the feature is available to you, try dumping your list into the chat box for it and it might just help you out with those exact songs. This doesn't guarentee a solution though because AI can make mistakes.
 
I hope that helps. If you need anything else, don't hestiate to reach out.

raw_text: Hello Spotify team, I’ve been thinking about something that could make Spotify even more useful and enjoyable. Many times, people hear a song playing around them — maybe in a café, on TV, or in a friend’s car — and they want to know what it is right away. Currently, we have to open another app like Shazam to find the song, and then come back to Spotify to listen to it. But imagine if Spotify had its own built-in music recognition feature that could instantly identify songs and open them directly inside the app. This feature would: Save time and make things simpler for users. Keep people inside the Spotify app longer. Help users discover and add songs to their playlists more easily. Make Spotify even more popular and self-sufficient.  I believe this would be a small step technically, but a big improvement in user experience. I really hope you’ll consider adding this to future versions of Spotify.  Thank you,Tural

2015-05-27
Hey everyone. We're marking this idea as 'Not Right Now' as we don't have plans to implement this feature. Today Premium users can use Shazam to identify music and use Spotify to play it. Just tap LISTEN WITH SPOTIFY in the Shazam app to open Spotify and start listening.
Users can also add SoundHound results to a Spotify playlist without leaving the app.

raw_text: I often hear songs playing around me and would love to identify them directly within Spotify. At the moment, I need to use third-party apps like Shazam, then return to Spotify to search for the track. It would be far more seamless if Spotify could integrate a built-in song identification tool that:• Recognises songs playing nearby and checks if they are available in Spotify’s catalogue.• If the song is available, it automatically adds it to my identified music library.• If the song is not available, it notifies me (similar to how Netflix informs users when a movie isn’t in its library) and suggests similar tracks or playlists that match the same style or taste.This would ensure that users stay within Spotify’s ecosystem, while also discovering new music that aligns with their preferences.

title: Bring a Built-in Shazam Feature to Spotify

raw_text: This feature is a widely requested and anticipated idea for Spotify users, but doesn't seem to have received much attention from the devs.There are popular alternatives like Shazam, Google hum search, or even aha-music finder. Though all of these have their own issues, like shazam being terrible for hum recognition, Google's ai model isn't 100% reliable etc.Having an integrated In-App feature within and supported by spotify would come with huge advantages other than just more handy:1) Firstly, since it would be supported directly by Spotify, it would have access to the users data. This is super helpful when trying to find a song for the following reasons. When you have a song stuck in your head, you have most likely heard it from somewhere - or it's a genre you like. Spotify knows your type of music and can prioritize them, on top of that (I find myself often listening to a song, then minutes later humming it but forgetting that I just listened to it and not knowing where it was from and what it was), so it can also check against your recent history for matches.2) Spotify is already the provider of music, unlike Google or other services that need to create a data base or use others, Spotify can check against it's own, meaning two things. Development is way easier, and also these days, there are quite a few artists I like that only upload to Spotify, meaning their songs do not often appear on other databases.3) Finally, I'm not suggesting it should be used as so, but it could be used as massive incentive to upgrade to premium or even a knew plan (for some features. I think the basic tool should be free). And since Spotify is very wealthy, they have the capability to build the structures, and make it great (maybe even knew discoveries for music recognition patterns). They may even be able to get into the ring with shazam and compete, offering API's to other buisnesses.All in all, I think it would be a great quality of life improvement for spotify and be widely appreciated by everyone. So I think they should at least try.

raw_text: Bring a Built-in Shazam Feature to Spotify I would love to see a built-in music recognition feature in Spotify, similar to Shazam.Many times we hear a song playing around us, whether it's in a café, on social media, in a movie, or on the radio. Currently, users have to switch to another app like Shazam to identify the song and then return to Spotify to listen to it. A built-in solution would make this process much faster and more convenient.Why this would be useful:Identify songs without leaving Spotify.Create a smoother and faster user experience.Allow users to save recognized songs directly to Liked Songs or any playlist.Keep users within the Spotify app instead of relying on third-party services.Improve music discovery.Suggested features:Add a "Recognize Song" button in the Search tab.Detect music playing nearby.Optionally support humming recognition.After recognition, allow users to:Play the song.Add it to Liked Songs.Add it to a playlist.View the artist and album.Keep a history of recognized songs.Spotify is already one of the best platforms for music discovery. Integrating a built-in song recognition feature would make the experience even more seamless by eliminating the need to switch between multiple apps.If you think this would be a valuable addition, please vote for this idea.Thank you for considering this suggestion.

raw_text: Hello Spotify Product Team,I would like to suggest expanding Spotify’s AI capabilities into a fully conversational personal DJ and music assistant.The idea is to let users control playback and create or edit playlists using detailed natural-language instructions. For example, a user could say:“Play this album, but begin with these three songs in this specific order, then continue with the rest of the album.”“Create a two-hour playlist that starts with upbeat 1980s music, transitions into modern alternative rock, and ends with mellow acoustic songs.”“Take my current playlist, remove the slower songs, add more bass-driven tracks, and reorganize it so the energy builds gradually.”The assistant could understand specific albums, songs, artists, moods, genres, eras, tempo, energy levels, transitions, and exact track order. Users could also modify the queue conversationally while listening instead of manually searching for and rearranging songs.Another useful feature would be the ability to convert playlists into prompts—and prompts back into playlists.Spotify could analyze an existing playlist and generate a description of its musical identity, such as:“Bass-driven alternative rock with 1980s influences, energetic opening tracks, and a gradual transition into atmospheric electronic music.”The user could then edit that description and ask Spotify to create a new playlist from it.In simple terms:Prompt → PlaylistPlaylist → PromptThis would transform Spotify from a music-search platform into a genuinely interactive personal music director. It could be especially useful while driving, working, exercising, hosting events, or anytime users want precise musical control without repeatedly handling their phones.Thank you for considering the idea. I believe it could build naturally upon Spotify’s existing AI DJ while giving listeners significantly more creativity and control.Best,Leo Douglas

raw_text: Hopefully a simple search feature, but I have soooo many playlists with many of the same songs in certain playlists. I'm a serial playlist creator. I sometimes know the song but I cannot find the playlist it's a part of unless I try to add it to a new playlist, in which case I'll see what previous playlists contain that song. The reason why this would be helpful is because some of my playlists that I create are curated by vibe, and instead of endless queues or searching through my playlists. I would rather find old playlists more easily by searching for a song that I know is in one of my handful playlists. It's like rediscovering old clothes and falling in love with them again.

raw_text: Hi there,Would like to propose this again as it looks like related tickets were closed and not addressed.Just want to share a concrete example on why this would be helpful... Throughout the day I'm listening to either music (while working) OR podcasts (while commuting). If I'm commuting to work, I'd like to queue up podcasts to listen to while commuting. This includes coming back to the same podcast queue when I'm commuting home from work (ie. same queue in the morning and evening). The problem is, I listen to music during the day while I'm working. So I need to clear my podcast queue and then queue everything up again after work. I think fixing this would increase user engagement on your apps. For example, having my podcasts on Apple podcasts is just more convenient now because I don't need to deal with this daily inconvenience. Every week, that's multiple hours of engagement per user (for a subset of podcast listeners) that you could be gaining back.I'm a huge fan of Spotify, so I'd love to be able to seamlessly use your app over Apple Podcasts for this use case. Thanks

raw_text:
Hey @IJustWantMyMusic, Thank you for reaching out to the Community and welcome. We appreciate all the troubleshooting you've done so far. In this type of case, since you're on the free tier, we'd first recommend turning off any active VPN as changing the country may affect the proper behavior of the app. If you're traveling, keep in mind that you can only use a free account abroad for 14 days, before having to update your country. So, if you're not using a VPN, we'd recommend making sure that the country registered on your Account page matches with the one you're currently located in and if it doesn't, update it by following the steps mentioned here.  Hope this helps. Cheers.

raw_text: With the rise of AI Technology, there are several artists appearing on Spotify that seem to be "AI Artists" Typically these artists have the following features that give them away: 1. A very high output of music, normally starting in 2023. 2. Nearly always remixes of popular Chart Tracks, normally done in whatever style the artist is. i.e. DnB3. Generic AI Generated Album Artwork / Biography Pictures Examples:  https://open.spotify.com/artist/7JlEOiSioeM2v6lPV6NDUn?si=ZGQkEWE0T2env03EqtGXuw https://open.spotify.com/artist/1C0giDNDAD8nKcIrxoENuD?si=yVqqj5WoR_mPk1XwqaZLUQ Currently, they are easy to spot, but in cases of Discover Weekly or generated playlists there is no option to remove or block these artists. As AI develops it may become impossible to distinguish between AI and Human.  To combat this, I suggest: 1. Spotify requires artists to declare if the music is AI generated. (If they declare it isn't, but can be proven that it is, they should be removed and banned) - Including retrospective questioning for some suspicious-looking artists.  2. Users have an option to hide / block / don't play AI generated music.  As a personal opinion, the music produced by these bots is low quality, possibly infringes copyrights? and detracts generally from real artists who are out there producing good quality music.

raw_text: AI music, like Suno creations, opens up my own music world with my own lyrics and my own compositions. However, on Spotify I usualy try to skip all AI-generated music in Discover Weekly or Release Radar — there should really be a default option to skip AI songs. Right now it feels like half of the new songs are AI-made, but in AI music I only want to enjoy my own, not others’ creations.It would be great if Spotify introduced three lists:Discover Weekly (only human-made music)Discover Weekly + AI (mixed content)Discover AI (only AI-generated music)The last option is critical for Spotify to remain competitive in the market, because Suno already takes up half of my music listening time — and I’m sure I’m not the only one. If Spotify doesn’t acquire an existing AI music project or start its own, they could be out of the market within a few years.

raw_text: Discover Weekly was a differentiating feature. No one else had anything quite like it. It was valuable enough for me to overlook the fact that other platforms may treat artists more ethically, provide higher-quality streams, and/or integrate better with my tech. Arguably, I should have given more thought to the ethics, but Discover Weekly led me to many new artists I wouldn't have discovered otherwise. I could (somewhat) justify the fact that, while they were getting paid more on other platforms, at least they were gaining a wider audience from Discover Weekly. Now that Discover Weekly is getting corrupted with AI slop, the value of Discover Weekly is evaporating, and the ethical questions are getting worse. 1) Discover Weekly is no longer a valuable feature for me, since I abhor AI music, 2) real human artists are being supplanted by low-grade AI music sausage meat, and 3) I'm unwittingly giving revenue to AI "artists" I don't feel should be compensated at all. "Clearer AI disclosures in the song credits, as well as stricter impersonation rules" is a quarter-measure AT BEST. If you have the data in the song credits, then you have enough data to build a switch for us to flip to turn off all this cynical, cash-grab AI music factory**bleep**. Burying this in the song credits accomplishes NOTHING. There are plenty of free tools to use to migrate playlists between platforms. Nothing appears to tie me to Spotify any more.

raw_text: The AI artists are not strictly a problem from the users end. The AI artists are also a direct way to abuse the Spotify royalty system. I took a look at some of the popular AI artists and there is clearly a common attribute all of them share. There are Spotify accounts, which seem to only exist for the sake of creating and promoting popular music playlists. A good example is "Vietnam War Music". The playlist mostly consists of popular Vietnam-era music, but between the real songs, the playlist creator has injected songs form a present-day band called "Velvet Sundown", which openly admits to being an AI-powered music project. A Modern AI-powered band should surely have no place in a Vietnam war playlist. Now you might wonder, what makes this so suspicious? Well, if you take a moment to consider the possibility that maybe the creator of the popular playlist, and the person behind the AI band are the same person. Consider how easy it is to create a viral public playlist, by using popular and trendy themes like the Vietnam war. Now consider how easy it is to create an AI song that gives off a Vietnam war -vibe. I have a strong suspicion that Spotify has recently become completely infected by people abusing user-created playlists, and a mix of AI music to abuse the Spotify royalties system. This is not a problem strictly from the users perspective. This is an issue that would not only affect users and artists, but also Spotify itself, since it has to pay an increasing amount of royalties to undetected AI artists.

raw_text: @jazzmanjohn Hey, I'm  sincerely happy you're not having to slog through the slop, I'm just saying it's probably not for the reason you think it is. The algorithms these services use do not *just* take your personal playlists into account when making suggestions, they use ALL available data from ALL users, this includes the bot-nets. If you listen to a song and don't skip, it assumes you like it, if you hover your mouse cursor (on a desktop PC, ask your Grandpa what those are if you're under 25) it thinks you like it, if you click it by mistake only to press the back button, it thinks you like it. See my issue is at work my hands are often full, so when Spotify sneaks me some slop into my discovery or personal playlists, I can't always skip it quick enough, so then it takes that lack of skip as my seal of approval for not only that track, but any other track even remotely like it, even if I then go back and add that one Artist to my blocked list later, the damage is done and I'm then stuck being force fed slop nuggets from $hitmountain from then on out. AI is kind of like herpes, once you have it, you have it for life. It's also entirely possible that you have had slop fed to you and you just didn't notice. This isn't meant in any ways as an insult, most people can't tell the difference, **bleep** even I, with decades of specialized training in spotting discrepancies in large scale data-streams, am starting to have difficulty spotting the slop, the divide is getting smaller by the day. PS: All career software engineers understand algorithms, it's baseline required knowledge. Also there's a couple dozen videos out there that specifically break down Spotify's algorithm and how it is manipulated from within and without for monetary gain.

Also there's a couple dozen videos out there that specifically break down Spotify's algorithm and how it is manipulated from within and without for monetary gain.
title: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs

I would venture to guess it would take your brilliant engineers one afternoon, and the only reason it wouldn’t is concerns over revenue. Scores of people will be discontinuing their accounts if this issue isn’t addressed in this manner. Please also address podcasts as a priority. It is disconcerting to hear something being passed off as Jim Rohn, who died in 2009, talk about dating apps.

raw_text: I would love Spotify to add an opt-in feature that helps users discover other people with similar music taste.Spotify already helps us discover music, but music is also one of the easiest ways people connect with each other. Many people bond over shared artists, playlists, moods, genres, concerts, and listening habits.The feature could be called AI Music Match.Users could choose to turn it on, and Spotify could create a simple music compatibility experience based on things like:shared artistsshared songssimilar playlistsfavorite genreslistening moodsconcert or festival interestsrecent music tastelong-term music tasteInstead of only showing “you both listen to the same artist,” Spotify could use AI to explain the match in a more human way.For example:“You both seem to enjoy emotional electronic music, late-night playlists, melodic tracks, and festival-style energy.”The feature could also suggest conversation starters, such as:“You both listen to darker electronic music. What is one track you would play at 3 AM?”Possible uses:finding friends with similar music tastefinding concert or festival buddiesmeeting people who enjoy the same music scenediscovering people through shared playlistscreating shared playlists between matched usersmaking Spotify more social in an optional and privacy-friendly wayPrivacy is very important. This feature should be fully opt-in. Users should decide if they want to be visible, what information is shown, and whether they are looking for friends, music discovery, concert buddies, or something else. Users should also be able to block, report, or disable the feature at any time.Spotify is already a place where people express who they are through music. This feature would make it easier for people to connect with others through that shared music identity.

raw_text: I’d love to see Spotify add a new type of playlist: a Public Curated Playlist.The idea is simple:Anyone on Spotify can discover and follow the playlist.Anyone can submit a request to add a song to the playlist.The playlist owner (moderator) receive a queue of pending song requests.Moderators can Approve or Deny each request to maintain the curation/vibe/theme of playlist.Approved songs are automatically added to the playlist, while denied requests are removed.Playlist owners can assign moderator permissions to trusted users so multiple people can help review submissions.Why this would be usefulCurrent collaborative playlists allow everyone to add songs directly, which can quickly become cluttered or off-topic. This feature would allow communities to contribute while keeping playlists curated and high quality.Some great use cases include:Genre-specific community playlistsFestival or concert playlistsWorkout playlistsGaming playlistsMood or study playlistsLocal music scenesPlaylists for discovering new artistsIt would create a fun way for listeners to contribute while giving playlist owners complete control over what gets added.Bonus ideasShow who submitted each song.Allow moderators to leave a short reason when declining a submission.Let users vote on pending submissions before moderators make a decision.Notify users when their submission is approved or denied.I think this would make playlist discovery much more interactive while still preserving the quality and identity of curated playlists.Include Spam Protection FeatureFor example, moderators could choose settings like:Limit users to 3–5 pending submissions at a time.Automatically filter duplicate song requests.Require users to follow the playlist before submitting.Optionally require accounts to be at least a certain age or have some listening history before they can submit.

This feature would allow communities to contribute while keeping playlists curated and high quality.Some great use cases include:Genre-specific community playlistsFestival or concert playlistsWorkout playlistsGaming playlistsMood or study playlistsLocal music scenesPlaylists for discovering new artistsIt would create a fun way for listeners to contribute while giving playlist owners complete control over what gets added.Bonus ideasShow who submitted each song.Allow moderators to leave a short reason when declining a submission.Let users vote on pending submissions before moderators make a decision.Notify users when their submission is approved or denied.I think this would make playlist discovery much more interactive while still preserving the quality and identity of curated playlists.Include Spam Protection FeatureFor example, moderators could choose settings like:Limit users to 3–5 pending submissions at a time.Automatically filter duplicate song requests.Require users to follow the playlist before submitting.Optionally require accounts to be at least a certain age or have some listening history before they can submit.

In making her name recognized and familiar, we lead others to  discover the reality in Palestine and humanize all victims. The project is self-perpetuating, organic and free.  The project includes a cover letter describing the project;  another attachment for historical context; and, a third attachment with how you can make the change happen.

raw_text:
I'm an individual developer building a small, personal music-discovery tool. Since the  recent changes, ALL write endpoints (PUT /me/following, playlist modify) return 403  Forbidden for my own account in Development mode — even with the correct scopes   (user-follow-modify) and a freshly created app. Read endpoints (GET) work fine and  return 200 with the same token/scopes.    I created a brand-new app to rule out an app-specific flag, and it returns the same  403, which points to an account/policy-level restriction. Extended Quota now appears to  require enterprise contracts, leaving no path for personal/hobbyist write access.    My use case is the opposite of abuse: I surface emerging, low-popularity artists  outside my algorithm and want to FOLLOW them to support new talent — fully aligned with  Spotify's mission of helping artists get discovered.    Could you please (1) restore basic write access (follow, playlist-modify) for  Development mode on the owner's own account, or (2) offer a non-enterprise path to  write scopes for personal projects?     Client ID: 4d5390831dd442f29c3f6b22cd48f030 · Endpoint: PUT /me/following?type=artist →  403 Forbidden.    Thank you.



					
						Solved!
					
					Go to Solution.
