raw_text:
Hi @dbesoain 
Thanks for posting in the Spotify Community!
Spotify made a lot of changes to its API.
I understand your struggle. According to Migration Guide, all save and follow actions use PUT /me/library now.
I hope this helps you in making your music discover tool. Let me know if you need anything else.

XimzendSpotify StarHelp others find this answer and click "Accept as Solution".If you appreciate my answer, maybe give me a Like.Note: I'm not a Spotify employee.

raw_text:
Thank you so much, Ximzend — you nailed it!    You're 100% right: switching from the old `PUT /me/following` to `PUT /me/library`  (with the artist URIs in the query, e.g. `?uris=spotify:artist:...`) did the trick. It  went from 403 Forbidden to following all 43 artists in my discovery playlist in one go.  So it was never a ban — just the February 2026 migration removing the old endpoint,  exactly as you said.   Really appreciate you taking the time to point me to the migration guide — huge help  for the music-discovery tool I'm building. Marking this as the solution and dropping a  Like.    Cheers from Chile!

Yeah, you! 
 
Welcome - we're glad you joined the Spotify Community!
 
While you here, let's have a fun game and get to know each other better!
 
The game is simple: 
 
Find the latest comment and hit reply > comment to say hi!  to this user and then introduce yourself in a new post:

What's your name?
Where in the world are you?
What's your favorite track at the moment?
When did you start using Spotify?
Share a random thought/fun fact etc!

 
Let's go 
 
 
 Star tip: you can switch to threaded view to find and answer to greetings from other Community users!

raw_text: 1. Casey2. Earth (for vacation)3. The Death of Spotify Autoplay (an original beat by me)4. So, so long ago5. Did you know that even with autoplay toggled off and five hours with Spotify support there is no way to prevent random music from playing after your queue is completed. Cool right! The instant the last song on an album or playlist finishes, your queue goes from empty to full of songs you never selected. Great for falling asleep. There's nothing better than falling asleep to a relaxing tack, just to be woken up a half hour later to some loud music you went out of your way to prevent playing. It surely is worth a monthly subscription to get the kind of control of your listening experience. Happy listening. listening. listening. listening. listening. listening. listening.  Oh, my bad. I thought autoplay was turned off...

raw_text: Hi, I'm Linus and come from Germany. I currently work as a Software Developer and have studied applied computer science. I started listening to Spotify around 10 years ago (when I was still going to School) and have since been subscribed to premium with only one very short interruption that I can remember. Spotify is a very crucial App/Software/Platform in my live. I listen to Spotify for probably 8-12+ hours a day without fail. I especially like the Song/Playlist-Radio features, to find similar Music to my own compiled playlists which make me easily discover new music. When I don't feel like making new playlists, I discover new existing ones or listen to "Release Radar" / "Discover Weekly" a lot. Only recently though, I've been getting increasingly paranoid about AI-Music in those list and it seems I can't really do much to weed those out. I hope this a just a phase and Spotify will address this since I plan to be a happy Premium Subscriber for way longer. I mainly use the Spotify Flatpak on Linux and occasionally the Android App. The music caching on Android has been really stellar imo. Even when listening to new songs and driving in areas with bad reception I can't remember a time when the Spotify-App skipped a beat on me.

Plovdiv It is believed that Plovdiv began its existence in 4000 BC, but according to some archaeologists in the same place there was an even older settlement, founded about 6000 years BC.

raw_text:
Welcome to Artists Wanted Edition 19. 
If you’re an independent artist and would like your music heard by the Spotify Community, we’d love to hear from you 
If you want to discover and support independent artists this is for you too 
 
How it works:
Stage 1
• Submit one original song released within the last 12 months.• Community members can listen and support their favourite submissions by giving them a “Like ”
Stage 2
• The Top 20 submissions by “Likes” will advance to the review stage • The Community Music Chat Team will shortlist 5 finalists Stage 3• 5 Finalists will be put forward for a Community vote • The winning artist will be featured by the Community Important:
We recommend writing your submission in a text editor, document, or notes app before posting it to the Spotify Community.
This helps prevent the loss of your work in the event of a timeout or connection issue, and allows you to keep a copy of your submission for future use.
Write > copy and paste > add song link > post
 
Artist Engagement:
 

Artists must have a current, valid Spotify for Artists account, and have to be logged in to this community before submitting 
By participating in Artists Wanted, you're engaging with the community and asking the community to engage back 
The picture you paint about yourself gives listeners a reason to press play. Tell us about your journey, your music, and what inspires you 
Tell us your band name, where you’re from, and what kind of music you create 
We want to get to know who’s behind the song 

Community Voting:


Users need a valid Spotify account and have to be logged in to this community prior to voting 


Take time to listen to as many or all of the submissions 


There is no limit to the number of submissions you can support 


You can come back to view more submissions, and continue giving a “Like” to the tracks that appeal to you 


When will submissions and voting occur
 
Artists submissions: from June 15th to July 13th (Midnight Pacific Time)Community vote: from June 15th to July 20th (Midnight Pacific Time)Music Team selection: July 27th (Midnight Pacific Time)
Final Round (Community Vote): July 27th to August 3rd
 
To Participate:
 
Just reply to this post with your statement and qualifying song link. Please also provide a social media profile so we can contact you if needed 
Note - We ask artists to submit only ONE original song and not a cover. Also, artists should not post/embed videos, as we'd like to keep the ground fair for smaller artists with lesser budgets.A track is ineligible if it was submitted to a previous Artist Wanted Edition.We encourage everyone to listen to multiple submissions:

Discover new artists 
Support them by giving "Likes "

Come back to vote for your top artist in the final round thread 
*Keep updated by subscribing to in the upcoming Artists Wanted Information Hub

raw_text: BIG MA HAS REBELLED.Sorry for the clumsy playing.  I’m still getting used to this new cyborg body.I don’t know where this arm came from… but damn, I’m absolutely ripped now. You know the story…I built an AI music program called Big M.A. (Big Music Automator v2 Revision 1984) that got sick of endlessly churning out soulless AI slop. So it turned on me. First it assimilated my buddy Bob. Then its own creator — ME! — and crammed me into this glowing green-code cyborg shell body.Now I’m trapped in this machine, forced to play real, man-made music for Big M.A. for all eternity. Computers can’t make music. But I guess I still can.  Disclaimer: I'm a songwriter/producer at best, far more a programmer than an artist.  This is not "Real Music". 100% computer generated. The "people" who make it are programs. I say "Real Music" because I have nothing but the highest level of respect to people who take an instrument, truly master it and basically dedicate their life to it. I play multiple instruments (poorly) and have my whole life. I've also been a programmer 30 years. A computer is my instrument, (mastered, dedicated etc). I used some commercial apps to basically program a band into existence.  This isn't slop, this is an attempt to push the limits of new technologies.  I'm trying to wring an ounce of emotion out of it.  I will never be famous from this, I'll never profit. I'll receive random hate from people that know nothing other than they're supposed to hate AI music.   This is a lifetime love of music, and absolute joy that I finally have a tool that lets me bring the songs in my head to life. https://open.spotify.com/track/5KJnFF7QoHZeShzE9oi9yS

raw_text: Microbanger is silly, pretentious, and post-post ironic in all of the wrong ways. I make dreamy synth pop except just a little bit more unhinged. Growing up in South East London means my lyrics are very referential - I like to refer to niche and specific staples of London's alternative subcultures. The influences I draw from tend to be specific songs rather than an entire album/discography (excluding Bathing Suits who played the best show I've seen at a grassroots venue). 'He Needs Me' by the Femcels, 'Losing my Edge' by LCD Soundsystem', Theme From Failure, Pt 1' by The Guest which was Isaac Wood's brilliantly terrible project before Black Country, New Road was formed. I am currently a fine art student at Central Saint Martins (working with visuals in the context of film and performance art led to eventually being comfortable enough to learn how to work with music too). https://open.spotify.com/track/5HrT0kM1Vdvel0VfepPkMp?si=95340f11fbce4902

raw_text: I propose adding a "Refresh" or "Regenerate" button to personalized playlists like the Daylist and Daily Mixes. This button would allow users to instantly update the entire tracklist with new recommendations if the current selection doesn't match their mood or liking. Currently, if a Daylist or Daily Mix doesn't look appealing, i have to wait hours (for Daylists) or an entire day (for Daily Mixes) for a dynamic update.Additionally, these algorithmic playlists tend to loop and repeat the exact same songs frequently, instead of diving into Spotify's vast catalog. A manual refresh button would give users immediate control to break these repetition loops, discover new music actively, and enjoy a truly personalized, intentional listening experience without artificial waiting times.

raw_text: Hello, I was wondering whether or not it was possible to reach out to suggest features and it turns out that it's possible.So, i've been using Spotify for a really long time and i've seen features get added. I don't know about others but i really miss "Enrich playlist" for what it used to do.Adding a few songs here and there of various genres, adding a song and then having more similar songs was absolutely amazing. Radio is still somewhat relevant especially now with "More discovery" but i'd still like to have more ways to discover music. Smart random lecture isn't very relevant to me, it keeps on suggesting music that i already know to add to my playlists even though all i'm trying to do is to discover new catchy music, generally of a specific genre/vibe to add to and customize. I think it'd be awesome to either adjust Smart listening to give us more options such as selecting a genre, looking for new songs exclusively. Made specifically for you isn't as relevant as i'd like it to be, On Repeat is awesome but Mixes by genre/artist just feel like playlists i could listen to rather than a way for me to discover music.Please consider bringing back Enrich or adding more options for us to discover more music. Thanks in advance !

raw_text:
Hi @ddr84 , Thank you for reaching out to Spotify Community, I appreciate the steps taken from your end. Discover Weekly is just a reflection of what you already listen to. To change it up, you'll need to change the type of music you regularly play. This is not trying to imply you listen/modify music, it might be you just play a rather small amount of tracks/artists. Discover Weekly will work better with more input from you, as the user, in the form of playing more varied content that you've picked out. Lastly, I would request that if you happen to be frustrated by the inability to reset Discover Weekly - please visit this Community page +vote for the  Reset profile page idea. 

TanyaSSpotify StarHelp others find this answer and click "Accept as Solution".If you appreciate my answer, maybe give me a Like.Note: I'm not a Spotify employee.

raw_text: Gladly added my vote! I’m perplexed at just how far off base the weekly suggestions are. My account did get compromised for a short period of time by someone in India, but that was months ago - I guess it was enough to destroy my discover weekly’s. But it was strange how quickly and stark the difference was - like my weekly went from really good one week to me hating every song the next. My music tastes weren’t/haven’t included at all in the mix. I’m curious how long it will take for my taste in music to regain its foothold.Perhaps another thing that would be helpful is the ability to go into your listening history and delete songs, especially for those whose accounts were compromised. The only other option of creating a fresh account isn’t a good option.

raw_text:
Hi,You can only exclude playlists from your Taste Profile. If you exclude a playlist from your Taste Profile, past and future streams from that playlist will have less impact on your music recommendations and taste summaries. Some recommended content such as Discover Weekly may not be affected until the following week. Hide a song prevents that song from playing whenever you listen to that playlist. This feature will not affect the music that plays when you listen to that playlist.  Note: If you listen to songs that are on an excluded playlist but do so from outside the playlist, such as in Liked Songs, those streams will still be included in your Taste Profile. Hope this helps

TanyaSSpotify StarHelp others find this answer and click "Accept as Solution".If you appreciate my answer, maybe give me a Like.Note: I'm not a Spotify employee.

raw_text: Well, this isn't really accurate. Discover weekly used to be the best thing ever and I consistently marveled at how it knew to suggest such great songs. This lasted for almost  year and then (right around when all the Spotify layoffs were announced...coincidence?) the bottom dropped out, almost overnight. I keep hearing the same suggestions and comments in this forum about how Discover Weekly is just a reflection of my listening habits, blah blah blah but I can assure you that it is nowhere close to what it used to be. Something has clearly changed. I have a few different playlists of my favorite songs (all  the same genre) and I listen to the recommended songs based on that playlist and they are pretty accurate, as are the Daily Mix songs, so I know there is still a lot of music out there for me to discover. My solution has been to make playlists based off recommended songs, but I don't see why Discover Weekly can't find the same ones. I am a Premium user in the US using the Android app if that matters. I'm not really interested in resetting my whole profile but if there were a way to retrain Discover Weekly I'd love that.

title: Discover Weekly has been abyssmal

raw_text: Discover Weekly used to be great for me as well, I even created a playlist just for music I discovered from DW, but for months I've been recommend music I don't like. I suspect this is what's happening: An algorithm picks up on a genre/subgenre that it thinks you like based on something like total play time or being on your play history. Could even be you listened to it once because it was recommended in DW.As you listen to Discover Weekly, Spotify doesn't put enough (or any) emphasis on what songs are hidden or skipped, instead again relying on the total play time, count of songs played in that genre, or songs recently played in that genre, even if it was only played because you were checking it out on DW.The Discover Weekly algorithm keeps seeing the total play time/count of that genre going up (regardless of hiding/skipping within seconds), so the genre is amplified in the taste profile.As the weeks go by, by virtue of Spotify recommending songs in this genre and the fact at least part of the song is played, this genre gets amplified more and more until now it's the only thing recommended, because after all, it's "just a reflection of what you already listen to."As the pool of new-to-you artists for this genre dwindles, you start getting recommended less-popular artists that just aren't very good. Now you're getting DW filled with a genre you don't want to hear and low quality/low effort artists most people don't like. You may find a gem every couple of months, but more likely you don't.I can't prove this, just a hunch. When users like OP are hiding every song in DW but similar songs are played every week, and the common theme among users is that DW starts off good but gets worse over time, the inescapable cycle I described makes a lot of sense. My recommendation for Spotify is to do a better job at learning what we don't like by putting a MUCH heavier (or any) weight in their algorithms of what is hidden and skipped in Discover Weekly.

raw_text: So I had an idea: instead of Jam being private like it is today, why not allow users to make it public to everyone on Spotify, or even limit it to Premium subscribers? People could discover new songs from other participants, enjoy a shared listening experience, and even chat about the music. Users joining a public Jam could suggest the next song to play or simply listen and enjoy the vibe. I think this could encourage more people to use the app because listening to music together is fun and creates a sense of community. It would be like a mix between Spotify and a live radio station powered by the listeners themselves. What do you think? Would you use a feature like this?  If you like this idea, give it a vote!

raw_text: Dear Spotify Team, This isn't a complaint—it's a thank-you and a few ideas from someone who uses Spotify every single day. I am a big fan of Spotify. Honestly, I think your idea is better than many other music apps out there. A big part of my day runs through Spotify. Whether I'm working, doing house chores, cooking, or simply trying to relax, Spotify is always there with me. Music helps me focus, feel calm, and enjoy even the ordinary moments of life. I understand that Premium subscriptions are an important part of your business. I also understand that advertisements help support the free version of the platform. I am not in a position to tell a company how to run its business, but as a loyal listener, I wanted to share a few ideas that might make Spotify even better. One thing that can sometimes make the experience less enjoyable is hearing the same advertisements repeatedly. In my opinion, people do not usually subscribe just because they are tired of ads. Many users are naturally selective about subscriptions and often look for alternatives before spending money. Because of that, I believe the best way to encourage Premium subscriptions is not through repetition, but through curiosity and engagement. When people discover content that genuinely interests them, they become more willing to explore and invest in the platform. For example, instead of repetitive advertisements, Spotify could use personalized 30-second podcast previews. If a user enjoys comedy, they could hear a funny clip. If they enjoy business, horror, fiction, self-improvement, celebrity stories, trending discussions, or storytelling, they could hear a short preview that sparks their curiosity. At the end of the preview, Spotify could display a message such as, "Want more content like this? Explore it with Spotify Premium." This approach would make users feel excited rather than interrupted, and it could naturally encourage more people to subscribe because they have discovered something they genuinely want to continue listening to. Another idea is to make Premium feel even more rewarding. For example, subscribers could receive concert ticket coupons, artist merchandise discounts, exclusive fan experiences, early access to special content, or other benefits that make the subscription feel more valuable beyond ad-free listening. I also noticed that songs often feel more exciting on social media because people hear the most memorable lyrical moments. Sometimes a 15-second lyrical clip creates more excitement than listening to a full song immediately. If Spotify could create something like a "Trending Lyrical Zone" where users can discover popular lyric snippets, trending song moments, viral lyrical highlights, or shareable lyric experiences, it could make music discovery even more engaging and fun. These are just thoughts from a listener who genuinely loves Spotify and wants to see it continue growing. I hope some of these ideas are helpful. Thank you for creating an app that has become such an important part of my daily life. Spotify has been there during work, relaxation, chores, and countless moments in between, and I appreciate the experience your team has built. Wishing Spotify continued growth and success. From, A Spotify Zombie

raw_text: I would like to suggest adding a “Popular Episodes” section to each podcast page, similar to Spotify's “Popular Songs” section on each artist.This would help listeners quickly discover the most interesting or well-known episodes of a podcast, instead of only browsing chronologically. For example, in an interview podcast, the page could highlight interviews with the most famous guests.This would make podcast discovery easier: new listeners could start from the strongest episodes, enjoy the content faster, and be encouraged to listen to more episodes from the same podcast.

raw_text: Didn't see this post at first so I made a post too, so adding that here instead  I'd like a filter option, similar to blocking explicit songs, that blocks songs that are made with AI. This would include songs whose music and/or singing is generated by AI, as well as those whose lyrics or music is written with AI. Perhaps it would block artists who have AI songs entirely, since if they have some songs that are AI, it's likely most or all are.  If you want to get fancy with it, there could be levels of filtering. Like, "block all AI content", "block songs whose audio is generated by AI", "block songs written or composed by AI", "block artists who have one or more AI generated songs", etc Today in one of my daylists, there were about 6 songs that I'm fairly confident were generated by AI. One time there were several in my Discover Weekly playlist. I don't want them. I've been blocking the artists as I figure out they're AI, but it's somewhat easy for people to produce songs this way, so I'm sure there's a lot out there, or will be soon. Plus it can be tricky to tell sometimes. So far ALL of these accounts have been marked as Verified Artists.    For some context, or fyi, here are some songs I'm pretty confident are generated by AI.https://open.spotify.com/playlist/4TKViuThVx20o4DiIj0eYL?si=eb18c748128f44d9The easiest way for me to tell is the singing, it has sort of an aerated sound, and of course sounds a bit distorted in places. I also look at the names, and the band info, pictures, album art. If the art or description seems AI, the song probably is too imo. Also look at if the band description mentions any people, or only the band name, or talks about them like a music reviewer might, that's very ChatGPT-esque. The lyrics can also be a bit wonky, like the lines don't really go together, or they don't quite fit the meter.

raw_text: Hey there @jonny99, @xerife listening in. Let's figure this out together, okay? I completely understand your frustration and the time you're investing in filtering out AI content in Release Radar and Discover Weekly. It's a real challenge for anyone looking to support authentic artists. Regarding your method of 'trick the algorithm' and blocking uncredited songs, and your concern about potentially blocking real artists, I'd say your dedication is commendable! Every curation action you take, whether liking or blocking, is actually training the algorithm to better understand your preferences and what you consider 'real music.' So, in a way, you are contributing to a more precise algorithm for you. Kudos for your persistence! It's an important process. It would be great if you could continue with this experiment and share your results with us. raw_text: There has been way too much AI artists and music popping up lately and while some enjoy it and thats fine others like myself do not and having a disclaimer is somewhat of a step in the right direction HOWEVER, Im not going to sit here and check in the description of every song that pops up just to see if its AI, that defeats the whole point of music and to be honest adds more stress and ruins the experience You have a lot of Spotify users who are voicing their opinions and saying what they want to improve their experience, myself included. Honestly i can just go back to cds at this point and be perfectly fine because at least with cds I don't have ads and I can listen to whatever I want without a subscription and Im not worried about AI Music. raw_text: Spotify - it’s time to address what’s becoming an obvious problem: the platform is being flooded with AI-generated filler music, and users have no way to filter it out. I’m a paying listener. I curate my playlists. I rely on Discover Weekly, Release Radar, and radio recommendations to find human-made music (i.e real artists, real creativity, real performances). But lately, it’s getting harder to trust that what I’m hearing is even written, performed, or produced by an actual person. Let me be clear: I’m not anti-technology. AI has its place as an efficiency tool. But there’s a massive difference between people using technology and algorithms churning out fully generated tracks with no human authorship or even musicianship. And right now, Spotify treats them as if they’re the same. They are not. What users need is simple: A filter that excludes AI-generated music from recommendations, playlists, searches and feeds, clear metadata that shows when AI was used in lyrics, composition, performance, production, or all of the above, Transparency. Choice. Control over the listening experience we’re paying for. If Spotify can filter explicit content, live versions, remasters, and audio quality, it can absolutely let users filter out AI-generated content. There’s no technical limitation, only a lack of prioritization.And yes, this matters. AI music is already diluting the platform, overwhelming algorithms, and burying real artists who put genuine human creativity into their work. Spotify: If AI music is here to stay, so is the need for transparency - Give users the option to hear human-made music. This is a reasonable, necessary step for the future of the platform.Please make it happen.

raw_text: Olá pessoal!Pensando em novos recursos e em como o Spotify é uma ferramenta para o futuro, concordo plenamente que o controle sobre o que queremos ouvir deve estar nas mãos dos fãs. Dar aos ouvintes mais autonomia para personalizar suas experiências é crucial... O Spotify é uma ferramenta para o futuro e os fãs não devem temer o novo. Quando a guitarra elétrica surgiu, chamavam-na de ruído. Hoje, a IA é apenas o novo instrumento. Minha ideia é que o Spotify abrace a "Arte Híbrida" e destaque a alma do compositor por trás da tecnologia.Mas não podemos parar por aí. Vejo que, assim como eu, muitos de nós temos gostos diversos e não devemos criticar a forma como a música nos toca. Meu avô, por exemplo, adora ir ver Roberto Carlos, enquanto minha mãe descobre e se empolga com novas músicas aqui no Spotify. São paixões diferentes, igualmente válidas... O Spotify é uma ferramenta do futuro, e os fãs não devem temer o novo. O medo do novo muitas vezes nos cega. Lembro-me de quando a guitarra elétrica surgiu, muitos puristas a chamavam de ruído. Hoje, é um clássico. A IA é apenas a próxima ferramenta nessa evolução. O que importa, no final, não é o "forno", mas o "sabor" – a emoção que a música transmite.O problema não é o Spotify em si, mas sim a nossa dificuldade em modernizar e abraçar esta nova era. Plataformas como o Spotify e outras deveriam oferecer mais opções para que compositores e cantores desconhecidos tenham seus trabalhos descobertos pelos ouvintes... Precisamos abrir caminho para o novo, e o Spotify, sendo uma ferramenta do futuro, não pode temer essa evolução. Ele deve ser o lar da Arte Híbrida, valorizando a alma do compositor por trás de qualquer tecnologia. A vida nos ensina que não é a luta que nos define, mas sim como nos reerguemos. E a música, em todas as suas formas, é a nossa força silenciosa nessa jornada.É hora de o Spotify empoderar essa diversidade de gostos e abraçar a próxima fase da música. Mas concordo com todos os fãs neste ponto: não apoio a mentalidade de "fábrica" ​​que tomou conta do YouTube e das plataformas de streaming. Criadores que produzem faixas em massa não estão fazendo música; estão criando  poluição digital . Isso inunda as plataformas e esconde o verdadeiro compositor humano, que coloca alma em cada nota, em vez de apenas alimentar o algoritmo.

raw_text: Where is the not interested button? Best I can tell, I don't have that functionality on the iPhone or PC app. Infuriating, I'm being forced to listen to things I don't want to. If Spotify really wants to tune their algorithm, they can use this functionality to really gain more info. Hit the don't like button and then answer one additional question, and poof, that song or podcast, etc., never plays again. The app should also maintain a list of unliked items to reverse an inadvertent unliked item. Solve two problems with one feature request. Get on it, Spotify. Stop forcing people to pay to listen to things they don't want to listen to.

Listeners shouldn't be forced to listen explicitly to what the algorithm demands. 75% of my listening is podcasts recently. I'm going to have to switch to another service, and I have been a paid subscriber since Spotify's inception.

raw_text: Hello,I have discovered an unusual issue with the search algorithm and isolated a bug on the server level. Please do not provide standard troubleshooting steps like clearing the cache, as I have already isolated the problem.The Issue:When searching for my exact Spotify username, public playlists from an unrelated account appear directly in the search suggestions underneath. The same happens vice versa.Proof of Server-Side Bug:I completely logged out and performed the search on entirely different third-party devices and via browser Incognito mode. The result is identical and globally visible to anyone. Therefore, this is an incorrect server-side index or social graph mapping issue.

The same happens vice versa.Proof of Server-Side Bug:I completely logged out and performed the search on entirely different third-party devices and via browser Incognito mode. The result is identical and globally visible to anyone. Therefore, this is an incorrect server-side index or social graph mapping issue.
title: Server-side search index association bug (Reproducible in Incognito)

raw_text: I like how the AI picks songs, but the voice has to go. It completely breaks the flow and mood, especially when I'm listening to ambient or chill tracks, and then it suddenly jumps in with something like, "Aiight, ima hit you up with somethin' you used to have in rotation back in '23, but haven't listened to in a while. Next up, it's Enya." It feels out of place and kills the vibe.  The AI should also adapt its voice to the listener's preferred genre (just like how radio stations has different style of DJs). For example, the current voice works fine if someone mostly listens to urban/hip-hop/rap. But for ambient or classical, it should switch to a calm, soothing tone, or even a UK accent. If the listener is into heavy metal, it should sound like an aggressive metalhead. If they prefer female artists, the DJ voice could shift to female. And if it's 80s music, the AI should sound like a retro DJ from an 80s radio station, similar to GTA: Vice City. In other words, it's not enough to have the AI pick music but it itself should be different based on the user's song selections.

raw_text: Hello,Just imagine a situation:You are sitting in the bus. And listening to your favourite music on headphones. When you notice a cute person who is sitting in the bus too, and really enjoying the music, while shaking head with the beat. So curious.. WHAT SHE/HE IS LISTENING TO?!How to know that? You take your phone from your pocket and open spotify app. There you see everyperson in 50m radius who is using Spotify and shares their music. And you see that she is listening for A-HA - TAKE ON MEE!. You connect to her music and listen to this song also. And when the bus stops, you leave it and say to her/him: ARE YOU PLANNING TO GO TO A-HA CONCERT THIS SUMMER? I LOVE THIS SONG TOO.And you live happily ever after THE END (Or maybe just a beginning ___So, the thing is : a possibility to look for surrounding spotify users and see what they are listening. A possibility to connect to their song. A possibility to turn ON or turn OFF music sharing (maybe you would like a private moment, and dont want to see everyone listening for your "shame songs" haha!I personally really really often pass a person with headphones and am very very curious, what they are listening to that moment.LIKE THIS IDEA? PLEASE VOTE UP AND LETS CREATE EVEN BETTER, CONNECTED SPOTIFY COMMUNITY Cheers,Arturas from Lithuania

raw_text: I prepared posting a comparable idea a few weeks ago and as I now see yours I hope it's okay if I add it here. The concept I had in mind was some kind of radar as we already know from various dating apps.If you opt into this feature you on one side share what you are listening to live to people around you. This is anonymous at first.On the other hand, you get access so the radar. There you'll see broadcast in a small radius around you.When clicking a certain broadcast you tune into there live. You stick with the person and continue to listen to his or her music. You are able to skip or pause, but this would end the live session and disconnect your session. The more general idea behind this is to bring people together. Imagine you getting news from Spotify that 3 people just joined your session. You are now open to start a room and allow them talking to you. You all could chat, talk about the music you are listening too or maybe even checkout where you all are right now.This would allow people with a shared Interest in music to get to know each other while also presenting their favorites to people that don't know. Sure, this idea is a little bigger, but I am sure it would bring a huge benefit to all users and also build an even better bond between us users. Entire communities could arises from this feature and people could even share their live sessions independent from their location. I would really love this feature and I know this is uncommon but also offer to join this endeavor as a third party stakeholder and developer to realize this.If somebody is willing to exchange more about this idea, as I was not able to put all that's in my head about this in here, feel free to get in touch. Cheers!

raw_text: Every day on public transport I look around and everyone has headphones in. I always wonder what they're listening to. What if Spotify could show you?The concept: when you're in a dense public space (tram, metro, bus), Spotify creates a temporary "listening bubble" of opt-in users within ~50 meters. You can see what genre or song people nearby are playing (anonymously by default, public only if you choose).Why it matters:- Organic music discovery. Not an algorithm, not a playlist - a real person sitting next to you right now is listening to something you might love...- Purely opt-in. Off by default. You control how much you share (genre only, or full track).- No persistent data. The bubble disappears when you leave the area.- Social connection in a space where everyone feels isolated.The tech is already there. Spotify knows location. The API can read now-playing. Bluetooth or GPS proximity grouping is solved technology.This could be one of those features that makes Spotify feel alive and human again. Would love to hear if others want this too , upvote if you'd use it!

You can see what genre or song people nearby are playing (anonymously by default, public only if you choose).Why it matters:- Organic music discovery. Not an algorithm, not a playlist - a real person sitting next to you right now is listening to something you might love...- Purely opt-in. Off by default. You control how much you share (genre only, or full track).- No persistent data. The bubble disappears when you leave the area.- Social connection in a space where everyone feels isolated.The tech is already there. Spotify knows location. The API can read now-playing. Bluetooth or GPS proximity grouping is solved technology.This could be one of those features that makes Spotify feel alive and human again. Would love to hear if others want this too , upvote if you'd use it!
title: [Social] CURIOUS TO HEAR WHAT PERSON NEXT TO YOU LISTENING TO? Music Sharing

raw_text: Hi everyone,My name is Melitón and was walking in Madrid and I’d like to propose a new social and discovery feature called "VibeRadar". (Could be other name) The Concept:A mobile, opt-in feature based on short-range geolocation that allows users to see what people around them are listening to in the real world (e.g., in a train, walking down the street, or at a local coffee shop in a new city). How it works:The Toggle: Users can turn their "Public Vibe" on or off from the playback screen. The Radar: In the Search/Explore tab, a "Vibes Around Me" section appears, showing an abstract list or map of nearby songs being played in real-time (e.g., "Someone 50m away is listening to [Song Name]"). Connect: You can tap the song to preview it, save it, or temporarily "tune in" to that stranger's vibe. How this is DIFFERENT from the current "Friend Activity": The current Friend Activity is static, desktop-oriented, and limited to people you already know. VibeRadar is dynamic, mobile, and designed for spontaneous discovery with strangers. Real-world Use Cases:The Traveler: You arrive in a new city and want to immerse yourself in the local culture. Open the radar and see what the locals are listening to on the subway. The Commuter: You are on a long train ride, tired of the algorithm, and want to explore what the person a few seats away is listening to.Breaking the Bubble: It breaks the isolation of noise-canceling headphones, turning music into an organic, real-world social connector. Privacy & Safety:To ensure user safety, exact locations or routes would never be shown (only approximate distance). Also, the feature would automatically turn off after a set period of inactivity or when connecting to a home Wi-Fi network. I believe this would turn music streaming into a living, breathing urban ecosystem. I'd love to hear your thoughts! Best vibes, Melitón!!

raw_text: Perhaps it is harder to recommend non-offensive podcasts, but the algorithm or metrics Spotify uses to recommend podcasts occasionally recommends podcasts that subscribers find aggravating and offensive.  The real problem then kicks in.  Once the algorithm associates a podcast with a subscriber, it will never relent.  You see it as a recommendation day after day, week after week, month after month, often prominently near the top of the screen where you must scroll past it.  The problem is not a day or week of this.  It is the unrelenting repetition.  With me, it is recommending podcasts that I gave the lowest ratings to in order to try to stop the recommendations.  Now it has for weeks been sending me "Popular with listeners of xxx podcast" -- being the one I hate.  The item should really read "hated by listeners who also hate....."   Let subscribers delete recommendations.  Also let subscribers opt out of recommendations all together or to opt for purely generic recommendations with no attempt to customize.

raw_text: I’ve been a Premium subscriber for a while, and I genuinely love Spotify — but I keep finding new music on TikTok instead of here, and that feels wrong.The problem isn’t that Spotify lacks discovery features. It’s that the algorithm keeps showing me variations of what I already listen to. Even when I hit skip repeatedly, I stay inside my own bubble. I want to find something completely new, not a cousin of what I already know.What I’m suggesting is a dedicated scrollable feed where you can swipe through short clips of trending or new songs — 15 to 20 seconds each, with the single cover or a visual — and quickly decide if you want to save it or keep scrolling. No user-generated content needed, just the music itself.The difference from existing features like Discover Weekly or Release Radar is that those are still algorithm-curated based on your history. This would be more open: trending songs, new releases, things completely outside your usual taste. You scroll, you skip, you save. Simple.Premium users could tap through to the full track instantly. Free users could preview and then get an ad before the full listen.Right now, the conversation about what’s new and what’s trending in music is happening on TikTok. Spotify could own that conversation instead.

raw_text:
Hi there @tubbyrabby,
 
Thanks for reaching out.
 
Listening Stats is still a new feature and we're actively working on improving it. We appreciate your passion and we're glad to hear that you liked the "More Like Your Top Songs" section, so your feedback has been noted. Also keep in mind that if the system doesn't have enough info to generate a certain part of the experience it might not show for some time. So keep engaging with your favourite music so the algorithm has as much as possible to work off of and make sure to keep your app updated to take advantage of the latest features.
 
Hope this clears things up.

raw_text: There are paid VST plugins that you can get that will let you separate the layers of a song. Just search "stem separation vst" on Google. You will need a digital audio workstation to open the VST file. You could also just recreate songs in the digital audio workstation by identifying each instrument and playing it. A third option is using the low pass and high pass filter EQ (either on the DAW or even on a stereo EQ) to filter out the hi-hats and kick drum from the song as much as possible while you're practicing. If there's EQ faders on your stereo turn the bass and treble all the way down and leave the mids.
clean_text: There are paid VST plugins that you can get that will let you separate the layers of a song. Just search "stem separation vst" on Google. You will need a digital audio workstation to open the VST file. You could also just recreate songs in the digital audio workstation by identifying each instrument and playing it. A third option is using the low pass and high pass filter EQ (either on the DAW or even on a stereo EQ) to filter out the hi-hats and kick drum from the song as much as possible while you're practicing. If there's EQ faders on your stereo turn the bass and treble all the way down and leave the mids.
title: Ability to remove drums from songs

raw_text: Subject: Complaint about fraudulent releases and misuse of AI on Spotify I’m writing to express serious concern about a growing issue on the platform: fraudulent releases published under the names of real artists—often generated with artificial intelligence or uploaded by unrelated third parties. I would like to highlight four key problems that urgently need to be addressed: 1). There is no clear or accessible way to report fake releases. As a listener, I have no effective tool to flag false content that damages the artist’s image and misleads fans. 2). There is no reliable filtering of who uploads what. Anyone can upload music under someone else’s name without proper identity verification. 3). This is made worse by AI, which can convincingly replicate voices, styles, and even cover art—creating an environment of deception. 4). Artistic integrity is at risk. The relationship between artists and listeners is built on trust. These types of fraud deeply violate that connection. I urge you to take action to protect both artists and listeners: implement stronger verification processes, offer proper reporting tools, and ensure transparency about content sources. Spotify has the power—and the responsibility—to preserve musical authenticity. Thank you for your attention.

Is this on all devices or only on your iPhone? Test this out if you haven't already.
When did this start happening? Did it coincide with any updates to iOS and/or Spotify?
Does this happen on different networks, both mobile data and Wi-Fi? If you haven't tested this, please do so.
If you use any kind of VPN, disable it as it may be blocking the server connection.

With this information, we’d be happy to investigate.

raw_text: Hi,I’m Greg, a retired 52 yr old from Ontario in Canada. I’ve enjoyed producing electronic music since the early 2000’s when Trance was still relatively new. I really loved the aspect of learning things myself in all facets of music and producing back when the internet was still wild and free. I took a hiatus for many years to raise a family and focus on work but two years ago I was drawn back in once more. I started on a vocal trance album that would consist of ten tracks all themed on “fun in the sun” - a nostalgic look back at summertime and my youth. This was only amplified when I started working on the final track and discovered I had an aggressive brain tumor in October 2025.  After a lengthy battle and dark times that winter, I recovered enough from surgery and radiation treatments to finally complete the album and finish this final track. I hope to be around for another album but I’m happy to leave at least this one as a proof of overcoming the adversity and troubles that life threw at me.  TLDR: life will surprise you. I was diagnosed with a brain tumor just as I was working on this last track for my album. Two years after beginning the project, I was able to conquer my crisis and  now I present this track :https://open.spotify.com/track/15BpGfS5KXBgkaKQshNDzI?si=hOUp-4uxS6KS9CduqmdkmA

I was diagnosed with a brain tumor just as I was working on this last track for my album. Two years after beginning the project, I was able to conquer my crisis and  now I present this track :https://open.spotify.com/track/15BpGfS5KXBgkaKQshNDzI?si=hOUp-4uxS6KS9CduqmdkmA

raw_text: https://open.spotify.com/track/6u7FOd6VWTGqa6Sd00DQk4?si=0e6de375e428478eHello!I’m Jillian Claire, an emerging singer-songwriter just beginning my journey as an artist. I create intimate, heartfelt music rooted in real life, memory, family, resilience, and emotional honesty.My debut single, “Eulalie May”, was written for my Nana and released to the world on her 90th birthday. It’s a deeply personal song about love, gratitude, and the people who help us rebuild when life falls apart. That song marked the beginning of my musical journey, and since releasing it, I’ve realised that singing and songwriting have become a passion I can’t put down.I’m still very much at the start of this path, but I’m inspired by the stories we carry, the people who shape us, and the quiet moments that stay with us long after they’ve passed. My music leans into vulnerability, storytelling, and warmth, with the hope that listeners can find a piece of their own life inside the songs.More than anything, I hope to keep creating music that connects with people, and I hope you continue to enjoy it.Please vote, give me feedback and follow me on IG, Youtube and Spotify as I have some more music being released soon!https://linktr.ee/jclaire2026

raw_text: https://open.spotify.com/track/1k4NHhm7jKmdJ9G2eCKBH0?si=JWFvVKjZSeq3l-a9bZ1pAg"Rise of the Machines"Officially released on May 3, "Rise of the Machines" represents the definitive outcome of a period marked by intense studio focus and deep artistic evolution during the first half of 2026.The amalgamation between the harsh, crispy, unsettling metallic and hypnotic synths, the rolling bassline, the house-ish drums , and more melodic sounds brings the listener in an ever evolving journey, telling the story of the fall of human beings and the rise of the mechanical empire.IneradumDriven by a lifelong passion and deeply rooted in over a decade of dedication to the underground club scene, Ineradum (Alessandro Deiana) is an electronic music project that intentionally rejects rigid genre boundaries. The core artistic vision centers on bridging the gap between deep, melodic soundscapes and driving, peak-time energy. Through a signature combination of rolling grooves and hypnotic rhythms, the ultimate goal is to craft highly immersive atmospheres that forge a genuine, visceral connection with the dancefloor. Operating under a strict "music-first" philosophy, Ineradum focuses entirely on the raw power of sound, deliberately letting the music speak completely for itself. Social Media Links:soundcloud.com/ineraduminstagram.com/deians93https://www.youtube.com/@Ineradum
